schools.summary: Solomon built God's house first and hurried less over his own; the surest prosperity is founded on early piety, and God's honour comes before our ease.
Gill: the thirteen years against seven show a greater regard for God's honour than for the king's own glory or convenience.
This is the royal residence in Jerusalem, later 'the king's house'; the verses survey the several buildings of the palace-complex.
Named from its forest of cedar pillars; 100x50x30 cubits on four rows of columns, serving as an armoury (1 Kgs 10:17) and perhaps more.
Gill: not built on Lebanon but of its cedars and amid resembling groves; a summer house, the Targum's 'royal house of refreshment.'

debates.question: What was the house of the forest of Lebanon used for?

sides.position: a summer house or house of refreshment
an armoury / 'house of his arms'
an armoury and court of judicature together

themes: house of the forest of Lebanon

schools.summary: The forty-five 'fifteen in a row' are read either as three tiers of columns (Gill) or as forty-five side-rooms in three storeys of fifteen (Keil).
Gill: three rows of fifteen pillars on the second floor, beams above forming a third storey, roofed with cedar.

debates.question: Do the 'forty-five, fifteen in a row' number pillars or side-rooms?

sides.position: rows of pillars / colonnades
forty-five side-rooms in three storeys of fifteen each

schools.summary: Three rows of windows set opposite one another ('light against light'); Keil weighs the obscure Hebrew of 'square of beam' and the winding stairs.
Gill: windows on east, north, and south (none west, where the porch stood), ranged directly opposite through the storeys.
Henry: the hall was designed for pleasant prospects, windows ranged 'prospect against prospect.'
Gill: doors, posts, lintels, and windows were all square, a building of clean rectangular openings storey over storey.
Keil: the obscure 'square with the windows' is read as 'square of beam,' the openings four-cornered, window facing window in three ranks.
The pillar-hall (50x30) was a colonnaded vestibule to the hall of state, standing between the forest-house and the throne-hall, with its own porch and threshold.
Gill: the porch of pillars stood at the west end of the house.
Henry: the piazzas may have served as an exchange, guard-house, or waiting-place for suitors seeking audience.
The throne-hall was the hall of judgment, cedar-covered floor to roof, where the ivory throne stood and the king judged in person.
Gill: here stood the ivory throne on which Solomon sat to hear and try causes.
Clarke: this porch was devoted to administering judgment, which Solomon did in person.

debates.question: Was the judgment-hall a distinct building or continuous with the pillar-hall?

sides.position: a separate building, square, throne set among four central pillars
following on from the pillar-hall yet distinguished from it

schools.summary: Behind the throne-hall lay the king's dwelling and a separate house for Pharaoh's daughter, each with its own rear court; the exact plan stays uncertain.
Clarke: three houses in one building - the palace, the house of judgment, and the harem for the women.
Henry: had the queen's house been as near as it ought, Solomon might not have multiplied wives as he did.

outliers.note: Reads the honourable notice of Pharaoh's daughter's house as the first warning-note of the foreign marriages that would ruin the reign (1 Kings 11:1).

schools.summary: Costly stones sawn smooth within and without, from foundation to corbel and out to the great court; Keil parses the obscure 'coping.'
Gill: the costly stones were marble, porphyry, and the like - the best of their kind.
Henry: all the materials were the best of their kind, finely wrought even where hidden.
The foundation was laid with great hewn stones of eight and ten cubits; the great court, three rows of stone and a course of cedar, was made like the temple's inner court.
Gill: great stones of great price, laid as a footing for great buildings.
Henry: Solomon liked the model of God's courts so well that he made his own by it.
Costly hewn stone ran from foundation to roof; Keil defends the text and refers the 'porch of the house' to the palace pillar-hall, its wall like the great court's.
Gill: the whole walls were of costly stone from the foundation up to the ceiling.
The great court enclosing the whole palace was built like the temple's inner court - three rows of hewn stone with a cedar coping - and held quarters for guards and household.
Gill: the great court was the wall that surrounded Solomon's house.
Henry: Solomon modelled his own court on God's, the pattern of the sanctuary shaping even the palace boundary.
Hiram the artificer (not the king) is fetched from Tyre; a supplementary pluperfect note, his honorific title 'abi' meaning master, as the Chronicles record a 'man cunning to work.'
Gill: an artificer of Tyre, whom Clement of Alexandria calls Hyperon, sent at Solomon's request.
Clarke: an intelligent coppersmith of Jewish descent by his mother, probably married to a Tyrian.
Henry: with the head of a Tyrian and the heart of an Israelite; as the tabernacle was built with Egypt's wealth, so the temple with the wit of Tyre - God serves himself by the common gifts of men.
Reconciles Naphtali (Kings) with Dan (Chronicles) by tribe of father and mother; Hiram is filled with wisdom to work brass, echoing Bezalel.
Gill: rehearses the Jewish harmonizations of Naphtali and Dan, laying any error to the king of Tyre, not the sacred historian.
Clarke: Hiram was of Naphtali by his father and of Dan by his mother, so called by either.

debates.question: How to reconcile Hiram's mother being of Naphtali and of Dan?

sides.position: a Danite by city or descent married into Naphtali
of Naphtali by father, of Dan by mother
any error belongs to the king of Tyre, not the record

schools.summary: Two pillars eighteen cubits high, twelve in circumference; the Chronicles' thirty-five is read as the two together or including hidden base and chapiter.
Clarke: about thirty feet high; even now a founder could scarcely cast such massive pillars, solid or hollow.
Gill: harmonizes eighteen with the thirty-five cubits by counting both pillars together or allowing half a cubit for base or chapiter.

debates.question: How reconcile the eighteen cubits here with thirty-five in 2 Chronicles 3:15?

sides.position: thirty-five measures both pillars together
a half-cubit each for the hidden base or chapiter, only the visible part described

schools.summary: Each capital five cubits high; the three cubits of 2 Kings 25:17 is judged a scribal error, the Hebrew for three confused with five.
Gill: the chapiters were large ovals like a crown, or two crowns joined, set on the pillar-tops.

debates.question: Was the capital five cubits high (here) or three (2 Kings 25:17)?

sides.position: five cubits; the three is a copyist's error of letters

schools.summary: Net-work and chain-work like branches and fringes wreathed the capitals; Keil counts seven festoons, Ellicott judges 'seven' a corruption for 'a net-work.'
Gill: the checker-work resembled boughs and leaves, the chain-work fringes like those on the skirt of a garment.

debates.question: Is 'seven for the one chapiter' original?

sides.position: seven plaited festoons hung round each capital
an erroneous reading for 'a net-work for each chapiter'

schools.summary: The Masoretic text confuses 'pillars' and 'pomegranates'; corrected, two rows of pomegranates enclosed the chain-work above and below the capital.
Gill: Hiram adorned the pillars with pomegranates set in ranks about the capitals.
Lily-work crowned the upper part of the capitals four cubits high; the obscure 'in the porch' is weighed but not made to settle the pillars' position.
Gill: the lily-work was wrought in the form of the open lily-flower.
The 'belly' is the rounded base of the capital where pomegranate bands ran; a lost clause hides the full four hundred; Keil sets the pillars free before the porch.
Gill reports Lightfoot: the pomegranates filled the middle cubit, the belly of the chapiters, between lily-work and lower ornament.

debates.question: Did the two brazen pillars support the roof or stand free before the porch?

sides.position: they stood unconnected before the hall, not bearing the roof
many hold they were supporters within the hall (rejected)

schools.summary: Henry: Jachin ('he will establish') and Boaz ('in him is strength') were memorandums to worshippers to depend on God alone; their later ruin marked a forfeited establishment (2 Kings 25:13).
Clarke: the names mean 'He shall establish' and 'in strength'; emblematical pillars that supported no part of the building.
Keil: the pillars stood free before the porch, their office wholly significant, not structural.
Gill: they were set up at the entrance into the porch of the temple.

debates.question: Were the pillars structural or purely emblematical?

sides.position: emblematical, supporting no part of the building

schools.summary: The repeated notice of lily-work, explained by its significance, seals the account: 'so was the work of the capitals finished.'
Gill: the lily-work is repeated from the earlier notice and confirms it.
The molten sea: ten cubits across, five high, thirty round, its proportions near the true ratio of seven to twenty-two; it replaced the tabernacle laver.
Gill: called a 'sea' for its great quantity of water, a dipping-place for the priests.
Henry: where the tabernacle had a laver, the temple has a sea - the gospel makes fuller provision for cleansing than the law, a fountain opened (Zechariah 13:1).
Two rows of carved gourds (colocynths) ran under the brim; the 'oxen' of Chronicles is judged a scribal error for gourds, not literal ox-heads.
Gill: the knops were oval ('figures of eggs,' Targum) or gourds bearing ox-heads that may have served as spouts.

debates.question: Were the ornaments under the brim gourds or oxen?

sides.position: gourds; the 'oxen' of Chronicles is a copyist's error
gourds figured with ox-heads, doubling as water-cocks

schools.summary: The sea rested on twelve oxen, three facing each quarter, hinder parts inward, on a metal plate, their size proportioned to the basin.
Gill: figures of oxen in brass, of full proportion, bearing the sea.
Henry reports the conjecture that the oxen rebuked the golden calf - 'fitter to make posts of than gods of' - though it did not prevent later idolatry.

outliers.note: Cites Bishop Patrick's conjecture that the brazen oxen were set to bear water in deliberate contempt of the worshipped golden calf.

schools.summary: A handbreadth thick, brim flared like a lily-rimmed goblet, holding 2000 baths; the 3000 of Chronicles is a scribal error; it replaced the laver for the priests' washing.
Gill: the thickness was four fingers, a handbreadth of solid brass.

debates.question: Did the sea hold 2000 baths (here) or 3000 (2 Chronicles 4:5)?

sides.position: 2000, as the dimensions require; the 3000 is a letter-confusion

schools.summary: Ten square brazen stands, four by four by three cubits, panelled between borders and figured with lions, oxen, and cherubim, to bear the lavers.
Gill: seats, stands, or settles for the ten lavers.
Clarke: pedestals for the ten lavers to rest on.
The bases had panelled sides framed within borders and mortised at the corners, crowned with a pedestal; Keil rejects Josephus's three compartments.
Gill: 'this was the form in which they were made,' leaving the particulars to be unfolded.

outliers.note: Claimed each side was divided into three compartments; Keil rejects this as unsupported by the text and impossible for the size of the figures.

schools.summary: Lions, oxen, and cherubim (figures of men or eagles) were carved on the panels; below ran wreaths, and the base stood on four brazen wheels and axles like a wagon.
Gill: the cherubim, distinguished from lions and oxen, were figures of men or of eagles.
Four brazen wheels on plates, with undersetters; Keil holds the bases were mobile carriage-like vehicles, Gill that the wheels were fixed supporters only.
Gill: the wheels served only as supporters, not to carry the laver, since the lavers were set in fixed places.

debates.question: Were the bases' wheels functional or merely supporting feet?

sides.position: functional - the stands could be driven or moved about the court
merely supporters; the lavers stood fixed in place

schools.summary: A crowned circular socket rose from the lid to hold the laver; the verse is confessedly obscure, and Ellicott calls the rendering of 'mouth' arbitrary and the whole unintelligible in the AV.
Gill: a lesser base, a circular coronet hollow within, rising a cubit to receive the foot of the laver.

outliers.note: Frankly declares the verse 'most obscure, and in our version unintelligible,' faulting even Keil's rendering and offering only a tentative reconstruction.

schools.summary: Four wheels a cubit and a half high ran under the panelled sides, axles joined to the base, fixing the proportions of the wheeled chest.
Gill: the wheels stood under the earlier borders, keeping the parts of the structure in right relation.
The wheels were made like chariot wheels - naves, felloes, spokes, axles all molten in brass - confirming the bases as genuinely wheeled vehicles.
Gill: made in the same form and fashion as a chariot wheel, as the Targum also says.
Four undersetters at the corners, cast in one piece with the base itself, bore the weight down onto the axles.
Gill: the undersetters were pillars, the corner-supports of the structure.
The top of the base arched into a dome half a cubit high, from which rose a circular crown with holders and mouldings to receive the round laver.
Gill: the round compass is the chapiter, rising and widening half a cubit each to form the socket.
Cherubim, lions, and palm trees were graven on the crown 'according to the proportion of every one'; Gill reports Procopius's forms of hands holding a crown-like circle.
Gill: the word for 'ledges' means hands or handles; Procopius of Gaza saw forms of hands holding a circle like a crown.

outliers.note: Read the tops of the bases as sculptured hands upholding a coronet, the socket fashioned as hands holding a crown.

schools.summary: All ten bases were of one casting, measure, and form - ten exact replicas, five on each side, each bearing a basin of forty baths.
Gill: this was the form and fashion of all of them, the single pattern to which the ten conformed.
Ten lavers of forty baths each, five and five, for washing the burnt-offerings, while the sea served the priests themselves (2 Chronicles 4:6).
Gill: ten where the tabernacle had one, typical of the large provision in Christ's blood for continual cleansing.
Clarke: the lavers washed the victims for burnt-offering; the brazen sea was for the priests to wash in.
Henry: spiritual priests and sacrifices must be washed in the laver of Christ's blood and regeneration; we must wash often.
Five bases on the south, five on the north, the sea to the south-east between the hall and the altar - the court furnished in ordered symmetry.
Gill: right and left mark south and north; the eastward entrance complicates the reading of the directions.
The 'lavers' here are pots for ashes, with shovels and blood-basins; Hiram finished all the work, but the brazen altar goes unmentioned - an omission in the record.
Gill: these are the pots for the altar's ashes, the shovels besoms, the basins for the sacrificial blood.
Clarke: it is surprising one artist could design and execute works of such magnitude and variety in so short a time.

debates.question: Why is no brazen altar mentioned among Hiram's works?

sides.position: a simple omission in the record; the altar was made (2 Chronicles 4:1)
possibly David's old altar was retained (weighed and rejected)

schools.summary: The summary numbers over the pillars, the bowls of their capitals, and the two networks, each referred back to its fuller description; a small textual slip is noted.
Four hundred pomegranates, ten bases, ten lavers are entered in the tally; Keil rejects the Greek interpolation about forty-eight pillars as apocryphal.

outliers.note: Adds an apocryphal notice of forty-eight pillars of the king's house and the house of God, which Keil rejects as a spurious gloss.

schools.summary: A single line of the summary inventory: the ten bases and the ten lavers upon them, entered into the record of the temple's furniture.
The one sea and twelve oxen head this part of the list; the great pieces were cast in the clay ground of the Jordan valley between Succoth and Zarthan.
The stiff, marly clay between Succoth (east, Gad) and Zarthan (west, Manasseh) was fit for moulds; the ground is set in a landscape rich with Israel's history.
Gill: the thick, stiff clay was fit for casting brass, the moulds shaped in the ground and the melted brass poured in.
Clarke: Hiram found the particular clay proper for his purpose, perhaps near Bethshan.
Henry: the clay suited the work, and Solomon would not have such dirty, smoky labour done near Jerusalem.
The brass was left unweighed for its vast quantity; it came from David's spoils of Hadadezer and Toi (1 Chronicles 18:8), perhaps mined anciently in Sinai.
Gill: Solomon laid up the vessels in the sanctuary without weighing them, being so large and numerous.
Henry: the unweighed brass argues plenty and honest workmen; Solomon provided so much by care, not carelessness, laying up for posterity.
The golden vessels of the Holy Place: the altar of incense (presupposing atonement, figuring Christ's intercession) and the table of shew-bread (the bread of the presence).
Gill: all newly made except the ark, mercy seat, and cherubim carried over from the tabernacle.
Henry: one altar of incense, for Christ's intercession is one; but ten tables and lamps, the fuller food and light of the gospel.
Ten golden candlesticks, five and five before the oracle, symbolising light as the shew-bread symbolises life; probably added to, not replacing, the tabernacle's seven-branched lamp.
Gill: ten where Moses had one, denoting the greater spiritual light of the gospel church.

debates.question: Did the ten candlesticks replace or supplement the tabernacle's one lampstand?

sides.position: supplement it; the old lamp continued (as in the second temple, on the Arch of Titus)

schools.summary: The smaller golden vessels (Ellicott corrects the renderings) and the golden door-hinges or sockets, of a possibly inferior gold, complete the furniture.
Gill: the bowls were to keep the lamp-oil in.
Henry: even the hinges were of gold, that everything might be magnificent - some think, to keep the people from idolatry, though it availed little.

themes: nothing too small to be precious

schools.summary: The work finished in seven years; David's dedicated silver, gold, and vessels are brought in and laid up; Clarke infers the temple was built at Solomon's own expense, David's store reserved.
Gill: all completely finished in seven years, David's dedicated things put among the treasures of the house.
Clarke: Solomon brought in David's gold and silver only after the house was finished, so the temple was truly his own provision.
Henry: the surplus was laid up for the temple-service; children should not recall what parents dedicate; Christ is now the Temple, Altar, Light, and Bread.

debates.question: Did Solomon build the temple with David's dedicated treasure?

sides.position: no - it was brought in only after the house was finished; the temple was Solomon's own provision
